mission.title: Install and Configure MicroK8s
mission.description: MicroK8s is a lightweight, single-package Kubernetes distribution designed for simplicity and speed, ideal for development, IoT, and edge computing. It provides a fully conformant Kubernetes experience with minimal overhead, making it perfect for local development environments. Key trade-offs include limited scalability compared to full-fledged clusters and reliance on Ubuntu for optimal performance.

steps.title: Install MicroK8s
steps.description:
This step installs MicroK8s using the snap package manager, which is the recommended method for installation.

```bash

sudo snap install microk8s --classic

```

> This installs the latest stable version of MicroK8s.

steps.title: Add User to MicroK8s Group
steps.description:
This step allows your user to run MicroK8s commands without needing sudo by adding your user to the microk8s group.

```bash

sudo usermod -a -G microk8s $USER

```

> Replace $USER with your username if not using the current user.

steps.title: Verify MicroK8s Installation
steps.description:
This step checks the status of MicroK8s and verifies that it is running correctly.

```bash

microk8s status --wait-ready

microk8s kubectl get nodes

```

> Waits for MicroK8s to be fully ready.
> Verifies that the Kubernetes node is up and running.

steps.title: Enable Add-ons
steps.description:
This step enables essential Kubernetes add-ons like DNS and the dashboard for better functionality.

```bash

microk8s enable dns

microk8s enable dashboard

```

> Enables the DNS add-on for service discovery.
> Enables the Kubernetes dashboard for UI management.

uninstall.title: Uninstall MicroK8s
uninstall.description:
This step removes MicroK8s from your system. Be aware that this will delete all Kubernetes resources and configurations.

```bash

sudo snap remove microk8s

```

> This command will remove MicroK8s and all associated data.

uninstall.title: Remove User from MicroK8s Group
uninstall.description:
This step removes your user from the microk8s group, which is no longer needed after uninstallation.

```bash

sudo gpasswd -d $USER microk8s

```

> Replace $USER with your username if not using the current user.

upgrade.title: Upgrade MicroK8s
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you back up your configurations and resources. This step will upgrade MicroK8s to the latest version.

```bash

microk8s stop

sudo snap refresh microk8s

microk8s start

microk8s kubectl get nodes

```

> Stops MicroK8s before the upgrade.
> Upgrades MicroK8s to the latest version.
> Starts MicroK8s after the upgrade.
> Verifies that the nodes are running after the upgrade.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'microk8s is not running'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** MicroK8s may not have started correctly after installation or a reboot.

**Fix:**
Run 'microk8s start' to start the MicroK8s service.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'kubectl: command not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The kubectl command is not available in your PATH.

**Fix:**
Use 'microk8s kubectl' instead of 'kubectl' or add an alias in your shell configuration.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Unable to connect to the server: x509: certificate signed by unknown authority'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates an issue with the kubeconfig file or the MicroK8s API server.

**Fix:**
Regenerate the kubeconfig with 'microk8s kubectl config view --raw > $HOME/.kube/config'.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'no resources found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** This indicates that no resources have been created in the cluster yet.

**Fix:**
Deploy a sample application or enable add-ons like DNS or the dashboard.

versionNotes.changes: Added support for new Kubernetes features including improved scheduling and resource management.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated certain legacy APIs that were previously used in deployments.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from v1.30, ensure to review the new API changes and update your manifests accordingly.

resolution.summary: You should see output similar to 'NAME STATUS ROLES AGE VERSION' when running 'microk8s kubectl get nodes', indicating that your nodes are ready and running.

prerequisites.description: Ensure your system meets the minimum resource requirements and has snap installed.
